11 min read
Just now

--

Imagine you’re chatting with a friend on WhatsApp, searching for something on Google, or entering your name on a website. What do all these things have in common? They all use text.
In Python, text is called a string.
Strings are one of the most important data types in Python. Whether you’re building websites, creating chatbots, analyzing data, or developing AI applications, you’ll work with strings every day. Learning strings is not just another topic — it’s a skill you’ll use throughout your programming journey.
The good news? Strings are one of the easiest concepts to learn. You don’t need to be a programming expert. If you can read and write English words, you’re already ready to understand Python strings.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

In this article, we’ll learn everything step by step using simple English and real-life examples. We’ll cover:
➜ What is a string?
➜ Types of strings
➜ How to create strings
➜ Single quotes, double quotes, and triple quotes
➜ String immutability
➜ String indexing and slicing
➜ Built-in string functions
➜ String operators
➜ Common string methods
➜ Real-world examples
➜ Practice questions to test your understanding
1. What is a String?
Before writing Python programs, it’s important to understand what a string actually is.
A string is simply a sequence of characters enclosed in quotes. These characters can be letters, numbers, spaces, or special symbols.
For example, your name, address, email, phone number, and even a complete sentence are all strings.
A string can contain:

• Letters

• Numbers

• Spaces

• Special symbols
Now let’s see how Python represents strings.
Example
name = "Reddy"
city = "Stockholm"
phone = "+46 70 123 4567"
message = "Hello, welcome to Python!"print(name)
print(message)
Output
Reddy
Hello, welcome to Python!Every string in Python belongs to the str data type. You can confirm this using the built-in type() function.
Example
name = "Reddy"
print(type(name)) Output
<class 'str'>Notice that even phone above is a string, not a number—because it is written inside quotes. Anything written inside quotes is treated as a string (text) in Python.
2. Types of Strings
Python gives us several ways to create strings. Although they look different, all of them create the same string data type.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Let’s look at each type one by one.
Single-quoted strings
A single-quoted string is a string enclosed within single quotation marks
(‘ ‘)
Example
fruit = 'Apple'
print(fruit) Output
AppleDouble-quoted strings
A double-quoted string is a string enclosed within double quotation marks (“ “)
Example
fruit = "Mango"
print(fruit) Output
MangoTriple-quoted strings
Triple quotes (''' or """) let you write text across multiple lines.
Example
paragraph = """Python is easy.
Strings are fun.
Keep practicing!"""print(paragraph)
Output
Python is easy.
Strings are fun.
Keep practicing!Even though these three styles look different, they all create the same str type:
print(type('Apple'))
print(type("Mango"))
print(type("""Hi""")) Output
<class 'str'>
<class 'str'>
<class 'str'>3. String Immutability
Now that you know how to create strings, let’s understand one of the most important interview concepts.
Strings in Python are immutable.
This simply means that once a string is created, you cannot change individual characters inside it.
Instead of modifying the existing string, Python creates a completely new string.
Example
word = "Python"
word[0] = "J"Output
TypeError: 'str' object does not support item assignmentPython refuses to change the character in place. So how do we “change” a string? We build a new one:
Example
word = "Python"
new_word = "J" + word[1:]
print(new_word)
print(word) Output
Jython
PythonThe original string stays the same. new_word is a brand-new string. This is what "immutable" means in practice.
4. Creating Strings
By now you’ve already seen several ways to create strings.
Here are a few simple examples.
# An empty string
empty = ""# A normal string
language = "Python"
# Converting a number into a string
number_as_text = str(2025)
print(number_as_text)
print(type(number_as_text))
# Joining words together
greeting = "Hello" + " " + "World"
print(greeting)
Output
2025
<class 'str'>
Hello WorldThe str() function is especially useful—it converts numbers, booleans, and other values into strings.
5. Accessing Characters (Indexing)
Suppose you have the word “Python”.
How can you access only the first letter?
Or maybe the last letter?
Python provides indexing to access individual characters.
We’ll first learn positive indexing, followed by negative indexing.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Positive indexing starts from 0 on the left:
P y t h o n
0 1 2 3 4 5Example
text = "Python"
print(text[0])
print(text[1])
print(text[5])Output
P
y
nNegative indexing starts from -1 on the right:
P y t h o n
-6 -5 -4 -3 -2 -1Example
text = "Python"
print(text[-1])
print(text[-2])
print(text[-6])n
o
PNegative indexing is a quick way to reach the end of a string without counting all the characters.
⚠️ If you go out of range, Python raises an error:
Example
text = "Python"
print(text[10]) Output
IndexError
Cell In[64], line 2
1 text = "Python"
----> 2 print(text[10]) IndexError: string index out of range
6. String Slicing
Sometimes we don’t need just one character — we need a part of the string.
This is where slicing becomes useful.
Using slicing, we can extract any portion of a string with just a few characters of code.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The syntax is:
string[start : stop : step]
• start → where to begin (included)

• stop → where to end (not included)

• step → how many characters to jump
Let’s see how it works
Examples
text = "Python"print(text[0:3])
print(text[2:5])
print(text[:3])
print(text[2:])
print(text[:])
Output
Pyt
tho
Pyt
thon
PythonUsing a step:
Example
text = "Python"
print(text[::2])
print(text[1::2])Output
Pto
yhnA famous trick — reversing a string with a step of -1:
text = "Python"
print(text[::-1])Output
nohtyPSlicing never causes an out-of-range error, so it is very safe to use.
Example
text = "Python"
print(text[0:100]) # Python (Python just stops at the end)Output
Python7. Built-in String Functions
Python provides many built-in functions that make working with strings much easier.
Instead of writing long programs, we can simply call these functions.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

In this section, we’ll learn the most commonly used ones.
len() — length of a string
Example
text = "Python"
print(len(text)) Output
6str() — convert a value into a string
Example
print(str(100))
print(str(3.14)) Output
100
3.14max() and min () — based on alphabetical order
Example
print(max("apple"))
print(min("apple")) Output
p
aord() and chr()— character ↔ number
Every character has a number code (Unicode). ord() gives the code, chr() gives the character back.
Example
print(ord("A"))
print(chr(65)) Output
65
AThese functions are used constantly in data cleaning and text processing.
8. String Operators
Just like numbers have operators (+, -, *, /), strings also have their own operators.
These operators help us combine strings, repeat strings, and check whether text exists inside another string.
Let’s explore them.
→ Concatenation (joining strings):
Concatenation means joining two or more strings into a single string.
Example
first = "Hello"
second = "World"print(first + " " + second)
Output
Hello World→ [*] Repetition (repeating a string)
String repetition means repeating a string multiple times
Example
print("Ha" * 3)
print("-" * 10) Output
HaHaHa
----------→ in and not in Membership (checking text)
Membership operators are used to check whether a value or substring exists in a string.
Python provides two membership operators:

• in → Returns True if the text is present.

• not in → Returns True if the text is not pr
Example
sentence = "I love Python"print("Python" in sentence)
print("Java" in sentence)
print("Java" not in sentence)
Output
True
False
True→⚠️ You cannot add a string and a number directly:
Example
print("Age: " + 25)
print("Age: " + str(25)) Output
TypeError
Cell In[78], line 1
----> 1 print("Age: " + 25) # ❌ TypeError
2 print("Age: " + str(25)) # ✅ Age: 25TypeError: can only concatenate str (not "int") to str
Always convert numbers to strings with str() before joining them.
9. Common String Methods
This is one of the most important sections of the article.
String methods are used almost everywhere — in Data Science, Web Development, Automation, and Machine Learning.
We’ll learn them category by category so they’re easy to remember.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Note:-A method is just a function attached to a string, called with a dot: text.method() . Because strings are immutable, these methods return a new string—they never change the original.
→ Case Conversion Methods
Case conversion methods are used to change the uppercase and lowercase letters in a string.

• upper() → The upper() method converts all lowercase letters to uppercase.

• lower() →The lower() method converts all uppercase letters to lowercase.

• title() → The title() method converts the first letter of every word to uppercase and the remaining letters to lowercase.

• capitalize() → The capitalize() method converts only the first character of the entire string to uppercase, and the rest to lowercase.

• swapcase() → The swapecase() method changes uppercase letters to lowercase and lowercase letters to uppercase.
Example
text = "python programming"print(text.upper())
print(text.lower())
print(text.title())
print(text.capitalize())
print("Hello".swapcase())
Output
PYTHON PROGRAMMING
python programming
Python Programming
Python programming
hELLO→ Searching Methods
Searching methods are used to find characters, words, or substrings in a string.
Python provides the following searching methods:

• find() →The find() method searches for a substring and returns the index of its first occurrence.If the substring is not found, it returns -1

• index() → The index() method also searches for a substring and returns its starting index. If the substring is not found, it raises a ValueError.

• count() → The count() method returns the number of times a substring appears in a string.

• startswith() → The startswith() method checks whether a string starts with a given substring.It returns either True or False .

• endswith() → The endswith() method checks whether a string ends with a given substring.It returns either True or False.
Example
text = "I love Python"print(text.find("Python"))
print(text.find("Java"))
print(text.index("love"))
print(text.count("o"))
print(text.startswith("I"))
print(text.endswith("n"))
Output
7
-1
2
2
True
True→ Replace Method
The replace() method is used to replace a character, word, or substring with another character, word, or substring.
It returns a new string. The original string remains unchanged.
Example
text = "I live in Hyderabad"print(text.replace("Hyderabad", "Stockholm"))
Output
I live in Stockholm→ Whitespace Removal Methods
Whitespace removal methods are used to remove extra spaces (whitespace) from a string.
Python provides three whitespace removal methods:

• strip() →The strip() method removes whitespace from both the left and right sides of a string.

• lstrip() → The lstrip() method removes whitespace only from the left side of a string.

• rstrip() → The rstrip() method removes whitespace only from the right side of a string.
Example
text = " Hello "print(text.strip())
print(text.lstrip())
print(text.rstrip())
Output
Hello
Hello
Hello→ Splitting & Joining Methods
Python provides two important string methods:

• split() → Breaks a string into a list.

• join() → Joins a list of strings into a single string.
These methods are commonly used while working with user input, CSV files, text files, and data processing
split()
Example:
skills = "Python SQL Power BI"
print(skills
